caveats: a: The source covers all furniture manufacturing rather than 337127.
a: Custom millwork-like projects and standardized school seating have different task mixes.
rho: No industry adoption survey measures five-year implementation.
rho: School and public-sector customers may restrict data use or require auditable human review.
e: NAICS scope does not disclose independence or recurring program quality.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than directly observed.
s5: Cross-industry intentions are not completed industry transactions.
s5: Product-line and dealer acquisitions may not be coded as 337127 transfers.
q: No public contract corpus links productivity to price changes.
q: Specified proprietary lines retain more than lowest-bid commodity furniture.
d5: Construction spending includes buildings and many inputs beyond furniture.
d5: Enrollment projections extend older assumptions and school furniture also depends on renovation and replacement, not enrollment alone.
o: Operator requirement is inferred from physical product and institutional accountability.
o: Budget cuts, reuse, and imports affect d5 rather than being double-counted here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B and public-sector product revenue through catalog, dealer, specification, cooperative contract, bid, renovation, and project channels, priced per SKU or configured package with freight, installation coordination, warranties, discounts, and contract renewals.
lens.excluded_activities: Laboratory and hospital furniture classified as medical equipment, office furniture, store fixtures, permanently installed kitchen cabinets, furniture repair, captive internal plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of stock or custom furniture for schools, libraries, theaters, churches, public buildings, and similar institutions, supplied repeatedly through dealers, architects, contractors, purchasing cooperatives, public bids, and direct institutional accounts.

a.bridge: BLS describes processes and employment for the broader furniture subsector, not six-digit institutional furniture tasks or wage-weighted AI exposure. I map specification review, space and product configuration, bid response, design support, catalog data, forecasting, sourcing, scheduling, quality documentation, customer service, and administration into exposed wages; household, office, cabinet, mattress, and shade operations create two-sided error.
caveats: The source covers all furniture manufacturing rather than 337127.
Custom millwork-like projects and standardized school seating have different task mixes.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study at 10-15 eligible manufacturers, split between stock and custom lines, with current automation and hours for specifications, bids, design, configuration, scheduling, procurement, production, QA, submittals, and support.
a.rationale: Furniture production remains physical, involving cutting, bending, molding, laminating, assembly, finishing, packing, and handling. Institutional manufacturers also carry substantial information work around specifications, public bids, custom configurations, project schedules, dealer support, compliance, and documentation.

caveats: Construction spending includes buildings and many inputs beyond furniture.
Enrollment projections extend older assumptions and school furniture also depends on renovation and replacement, not enrollment alone.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price institutional-furniture shipments and backlog by end market, public educational construction forecasts, school renovation and bond pipelines, enrollment projections, cooperative-contract purchasing, church and theater renovation, and import penetration.
d5.rationale: Renovation, replacement, public construction, libraries, churches, theaters, and school modernization support recurring demand. Project timing, public budgets, declining school enrollment projections, product durability, imports, and reuse constrain growth, while aging facilities and accessibility or technology refresh support the upper bound.

caveats: NAICS scope does not disclose independence or recurring program quality.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than directly observed.
e.change_evidence: D&B or Data Axle establishment-to-owner mapping, then verification of external recurring revenue, dealer and cooperative contracts, customer concentration, backlog, normalized EBITDA, certifications, owner dependency, and equipment.
e.rationale: Institutional furniture is usually an external, repeat, specification or dealer-driven business with transferable products and accounts. Eligibility is reduced by project-only shops, captive units, single-contract dependence, owner-centric design or estimating, obsolete equipment, weak certifications, and firms outside normalized LMM control economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 337127 ($1,497,959,871) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 337127 ($5,061,328,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $244,108 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 337127); class <5: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $617,418 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $121,137 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,246,280 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $244,520 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 83 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,617,602 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $513,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 140 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,235,157 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,811,938 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,640,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,207,797 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $75,977,684 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,906,822 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 201

caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from physical product and institutional accountability.
Budget cuts, reuse, and imports affect d5 rather than being double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Institutional procurement shifts to local digital fabrication, manufacturer-insourcing plans, modular or software-defined furniture systems, autonomous production evidence, and outsource renewal rates.
o.rationale: Institutions still require accountable manufacturers for physical furniture design, structural integrity, accessibility, fire and safety compliance, finish quality, warranty, and delivery. Software can configure and procure products but cannot supply the current physical basket; occasional local fabrication or customer insourcing is limited.

caveats: No public contract corpus links productivity to price changes.
Specified proprietary lines retain more than lowest-bid commodity furniture.
q.change_evidence: Bid tabs, cooperative and dealer agreements, discount schedules, specification win rates, contract renewal and rebid histories, change orders, freight terms, and project or SKU margins before and after automation.
q.rationale: Specifications, certifications, product durability, dealer relationships, cooperative contracts, responsiveness, and custom capability protect some benefits. Public bids, purchasing cooperatives, architects, general contractors, and dealers compare prices, demand discounts, and rebid contracts, eroding visible savings over award cycles.

caveats: No industry adoption survey measures five-year implementation.
School and public-sector customers may restrict data use or require auditable human review.
rho.change_evidence: Pre/post deployment evidence for AI bid response, configurators, design assistance, submittals, planning, inspection, dealer support, and administrative automation, including error, cycle-time, labor, on-time delivery, and award-rate metrics.
rho.rationale: AI can be integrated into bid parsing, submittals, product configuration, document search, scheduling, catalog content, forecasting, and service. Public procurement rules, specification accuracy, accessibility and safety requirements, custom design, fragmented dealer data, and physical production change management constrain full implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year plans among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed institutional-furniture control transfers. I center on the observed 22 percent employer sell or transfer intention and allow for internal succession, strategic product-line sales, closures, distress, and transaction non-completion.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed industry transactions.
Product-line and dealer acquisitions may not be coded as 337127 transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of closed institutional-furniture transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, furniture advisers, and brokers, matched to a verified eligible denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: Private regional manufacturers can transfer with product lines, contracts, specifications, dealer relationships, workforce, and equipment. Owner dependence and public-contract concentration can instead cause liquidation, asset sales, or tuck-ins.

principal_driver: Repeat institutional replacement and project demand with document-heavy specifications, bids, configurations, and submittals suited to AI.
principal_weakness: Transparent public procurement and cyclical budgets constrain retained savings and demand visibility.
